pattern: A zero-free number's digit product only ever carries the prime factors 2, 3, 5 and 7, so divisibility by t collapses into needing at least a certain number of each of those four primes; searching for the smallest qualifying number then becomes a greedy digit-by-digit construction guided by a feasibility check over how many free digit slots remain.
hints: Factor t using only the primes 2, 3, 5 and 7 -- if anything is left over afterward, no zero-free digit product could ever be divisible by t, so the answer is impossible.

Given some number of totally free digit positions (each 1-9) and a remaining amount of each of the four prime factors still needed, you can check feasibility recursively: try every digit 1-9, subtract what it contributes, and see if the rest can be finished with one fewer free position.

First see if the given number already qualifies as-is. Otherwise, scanning from the rightmost digit backward, try bumping that digit up to the smallest larger value that still leaves the remaining positions feasible, and greedily fill those remaining positions with the smallest digits that stay feasible. If no same-length fix exists, fall back to the smallest longer zero-free number that can meet the requirement from scratch.

edge_inputs.description: same-length fix requires changing one digit and filling the rest
the input already satisfies the requirement unchanged
t has a prime factor outside 2, 3, 5, 7 so no answer can ever exist
t equal to 1 is trivially satisfied by any zero-free number, including the input itself
the input itself contains a zero digit so it can never qualify even though its product happens to be right
shortest possible input, needing a length increase since no same-length fix exists
small increment at the very last digit is enough
already at the top end of single-digit values per position

stress.description: random short digit strings with random small targets, some feasible and some not
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(2, max(2, min(size, 8)))
    num = str(rng.randint(1, 9)) + ''.join(str(rng.randint(0, 9)) for _ in range(n - 1))
    t = rng.randint(1, 500)
    return [num, t]

complexity.time: O(L * 9 * state space) via memoized feasibility search
